coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating Amino-Functionalized Fe3O4 and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots for adsorption and detection of Cr (VI)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Yong
Hu, Zhiyi
Lei, Xia
Wang, Yuanlan
Guo, Xin
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel (FMCH) incorporating Amino-Functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles (AF-Fe3O4 NPs) and cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) modified with carbon dots (CDs) was successfully synthesized via the crosslinking method. The FMCH was used as an adsorbent for removing Cr (VI) from aqueous solution and it exhibited encouraging adsorption performance for Cr (VI). The composition and structure of FMCH were characterized and its Cr (VI) sorption performance was evaluated. The results demonstrated that this hydrogel had 3D porous structures and a higher adsorption ability of Cr (VI). The maximum adsorption capacity of this hydrogel was measured to be 212.1 mg/g at 25 °C and pH 5.0. The performance of the FMCH in Cr (VI) adsorption was evaluated through a contrast experiment with the normal chitosan hydrogel without CNFs and AF-Fe3O4 NPs. The effect of the initial concentration of Cr (VI) and contact time were also evaluated. The results confirmed that the adsorption isotherm was according to the Freundlich model, and the adsorption kinetics followed the pseudo-second-order model. Furthermore, as a fluorescent sensor for Cr (VI), the detection sensitivity and selectivity were examined. The quantitative detection of Cr (VI) was achieved in the linear range of 20–800 mg/L. Meantime, the mechanism for highly efficient adsorption and detection of FMCH for Cr (VI) was analysed by FTIR and XPS. These results showed that the excellent sorption and sensing abilities of Cr (VI) may be mainly attributed to the porous structures which provided many active sites and ion transport channels and the additional AF-Fe3O4 NPs and CNFs modified with CDs improving the sorption ability of Cr (VI) and providing a rapid visual response to Cr (VI). It showed that the FMCH can be widely used for Cr (VI) detection and removal in wastewater.

row.entry: Fe3O4 NPs functionalized polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan magnetic composite hydrogel

head.ce:title: Fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating Amino-Functionalized Fe3O4 and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots for adsorption and detection of Cr (VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel (FMCH) incorporating Amino-Functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles (AF-Fe3O4 NPs) and cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) modified with carbon dots (CDs) was successfully synthesized via the crosslinking method. The FMCH was used as an adsorbent for removing Cr (VI) from aqueous solution and it exhibited encouraging adsorption performance for Cr (VI). The composition and structure of FMCH were characterized and its Cr (VI) sorption performance was evaluated. The results demonstrated that this hydrogel had 3D porous structures and a higher adsorption ability of Cr (VI). The maximum adsorption capacity of this hydrogel was measured to be 212.1 mg/g at 25 °C and pH 5.0. The performance of the FMCH in Cr (VI) adsorption was evaluated through a contrast experiment with the normal chitosan hydrogel without CNFs and AF-Fe3O4 NPs. The effect of the initial concentration of Cr (VI) and contact time were also evaluated. The results confirmed that the adsorption isotherm was according to the Freundlich model, and the adsorption kinetics followed the pseudo-second-order model. Furthermore, as a fluorescent sensor for Cr (VI), the detection sensitivity and selectivity were examined. The quantitative detection of Cr (VI) was achieved in the linear range of 20–800 mg/L. Meantime, the mechanism for highly efficient adsorption and detection of FMCH for Cr (VI) was analysed by FTIR and XPS. These results showed that the excellent sorption and sensing abilities of Cr (VI) may be mainly attributed to the porous structures which provided many active sites and ion transport channels and the additional AF-Fe3O4 NPs and CNFs modified with CDs improving the sorption ability of Cr (VI) and providing a rapid visual response to Cr (VI). It showed that the FMCH can be widely used for Cr (VI) detection and removal in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the discharge of heavy metals into aquatic ecosystems is a matter of concern in wastewater treatment due to their harmful effects on humans even at low concentrations [1–3]. Among these heavy metals, Cr (VI) is one of the most prominent hazardous contaminants, it can enter the human body and cause many poisoning symptoms and diseases [4,5]. Hence, it is greatly important to design and fabricate a adsorbent for Cr (VI) removal in today’s research. Up to now, many treatment technologies have been used in this regard, including chemical precipitation, ion exchange, membrane technologies, coagulation, electrocoagulation, reduction, biosorption, filtration, adsorption, reverse osmosis, foam flotation, electrolysis and adsorption [6–10]. Nevertheless, as a conventional technique, the adsorption method is still deemed as a feasible way on account of its economy, high efficiency and environmental acceptance with respect to metal removal. In recent years, adsorbents derived from biomass materials have attracted great interest of many researchers in the field of environmental remediation. Biomass materials are renewable, low-cost and available in the environment, often used to treat heavy metal ions from liquid waste. Some typical adsorbents used in heavy metal ion adsorption include activated carbon [11–13], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [14], bioadsorbents [15,16], and so on. Hence, it is necessary to develop new adsorbents that can be removed quickly and recycled in order to achieve a more efficient wastewater treatment system [17].

Chitosan (CS) is a naturally occurring linear polysaccharide derived from chitin [18]. Chitosan is an excellent adsorbent, which is rich in functional groups including amino (–NH2) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups that served as the sorption sites for metal ions through a series of processes of coordinated complexation, electrostatic interaction and ion exchange [19–21]. It is considered a useful material in water treatment for removing heavy metals. Zhang et al. prepared malic acid-enhanced chitosan hydrogel to remove Cr (VI) from an aqueous solution [22]. Sharma et al. fabricated chitosan-crosslinked-poly(alginic acid) nano-hydrogel using the copolymerization method as an adsorbent for Cr (VI) [23]. Vilela et al. prepared a chitosan-based hydrogel for removing Cr (VI), and the maximum sorption capacity was measured to be 73.14 mg/g [24]. These chitosan-based hydrogels showed great potential for Cr (VI) removal, and their adsorption properties were further enhanced by various methods such as blending and crosslinking with other preferable nanomaterials [25]. Among these nanomaterials such as TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (CNFs), carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles are expected to be used to enhance the performance of the chitosan-based hydrogel. CNFs, a type of green functional material, have many carboxyl groups, which are mainly derived from the oxidation of primary hydroxyl groups [26]. It has inherent characteristics such as biodegradability, good dispersion stability, high mechanical stiffness, ease of forming a network structure, and abundance of binding sites for heavy metals [27]. Furthermore, the CNFs can offer a natural skeleton for assembling three-dimensional porous structures and provide ample hydroxyl groups for facilitating the accumulation and adsorption of Cr (VI) [61,62]. Therefore, CNFs are a potential candidate to improve the adsorbent. Meanwhile, CDs have emerged as biological and chemical sensors because of their good fluorescence property, biocompatibility and nontoxicity [28,29]. The favourable fluorescence properties show considerable potential for new applications in environmental sensing, such as the detection of heavy metals. CDs can introduce fluorescence properties to develop new function for hydrogel such as detecting heavy metals. Many reports revealed that CDs can be potentially used for analysing Cr (VI) [63,64]. Therefore, the introduction of CDs can help the adsorbent to develop new function in analysing Cr (VI). CDs need to be dispersed homogeneously in the matrix, so they can be immobilised into a stable matrix (e.g. a chitosan-based hydrogel). With abundant NH2, CDs could be connected with the CS-AA skeleton of FMCH through hydrogen bonds, thus building a hydrogel network. Magnetic nanoparticles have attracted extensive attention due to their low toxicity, easy separation and high sensitivity [30,31], large surface area and great diversity in surface functionalization [32–35] which can be formed organic-inorganic composite hydrogels through blending with chitosan. The main role of Fe3O4 is in this paper to introduce magnetic properties for hydrogel, making recycling easier in wastewater [36]. The Fe3O4 mentioned here was amine-functionalized magnetite nanoparticles (AF-Fe3O4 NPs). It not only provides a large number of amino-functional groups for the hydrogel but also improve the gel’s mechanical properties [65–67]. Owing to the high surface area, AF-Fe3O4 NPs can agglomerate easily, causing much inconvenience during practical use. To reduce the aggregation of the nanoparticles in aqueous solution, Fe3O4 was encapsulated by amino groups and CS was used as a dispersion to stabilize the AF-Fe3O4 NPs via electrostatic repulsion. In addition, amino groups on the surface of Fe3O4 can reduce the oxidation reaction and then Fe3O4 can maintain its magnetic properties [68]. Because of these excellent characteristics of CNFs, CDs and AF-Fe3O4 NPs, the motivation of this work is to develop a chitosan-based hydrogel using these three nano-materials to enhance adsorption performance and add new features.

In this study, a fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel (FMCH) incorporating AF-Fe3O4 NPs and CNFs modified with CDs was prepared and analysed by SEM, XRD, FTIR, UV–visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and VSM. The adsorption behavior for Cr (VI) onto this hydrogel was examined. Equilibrium isotherms and adsorption kinetics were evaluated. In addition, fluorescence detection for Cr (VI) was studied, in which the detection sensitivity and selectivity were investigated. On this basis, the mechanism of highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr (VI) for this chitosan-based hydrogel was determined.

2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO), chitosan (CS), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), FeCl3·6H2O, sodium acetate, 1,6-hexanediamine, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), ethanol, ethylene glycol, and potassium persulfate (PPS) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. Potassium dichromate (99 %) was used for the preparation of Cr (VI) solution. The test products are analytical grade，not purified. Additionally, doubly distilled deionized water was used throughout the work.

The preparation procedure of CNFs has been reported, and we used the same procedure here [37]. First, 10.0 g of dried bleached wood pulp was dispersed in 350 mL of distilled water. NaBr (0.4000 g), NaClO (6.0 g) and TEMPO (0.0800 g) were added sequentially under mechanical stirring. Once the pH value of the mixture became 11.0, the oxidation reaction started. After 6 h, 60 mL of ethanol was added to stop the reaction. Subsequently, the mixture was washed with distilled water to neutral pH and then passed through a high pressure homogenizer six times. Next, the CNFs were prepared and dialyzed for 4 days. Finally, the CNFs were diluted to 1.0 wt. %.

The CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method [38]. First, dissolve citric acid (4.2 g) in distilled water (100 mL), add ethylenediamine (5.36 mL), stir at room temperature for 2 h, then put the mixture into a Teflon-lined pressure vessel (150 mL), heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature, a resulting dark brown solution was obtained. The product was filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous filter to remove large particles and dialyzed with a dialysis membrane for 2 d. Then, the CDs were obtained.

CNFs-CDs were prepared via a condensation reaction [39]. The CNFs suspension (10.0 mL, 1.0 wt. %) was stirred in acetate buffer solution (2.5 mL, pH = 3.5) continuously at 40 °C. Then, EDC (30 mg) and NHS (120 mg) were added to the CNFs suspension and mixed uniformly. Thereafter, CDs solution with different volumes (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, 1.2 and 1.6 mL) was added.

Later, the mixture was stirred at 25 °C in a dark place for 12 h to form CNFs-CDs. The products were labelled as CNFs-CDs-1, CNFs-CDs-2, CNFs-CDs-3, CNFs-CDs-4, CNFs-CDs-5, CNFs-CDs-6, CNFs-CDs-7, CNFs-CDs-8 and CNFs-CDs-9, respectively.

AF-Fe3O4 NPs were prepared by a solvothermal method [40]. During this process, a solution of anhydrous sodium acetate (6.0 g), 1,6-hexanediamine (18.0 g) and FeCl3·6 H2O (3.0 g) as a single Fe3+ ion source was added to ethylene glycol (120 mL). The above mixture was vigorously stirred at 50 °C for 30 min. This solution was then heated at 200 °C in a Teflon-lined autoclave for 6 h. After that, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. The magnetite nanoparticles were collected with a magnet and were then washed with deionized water and ethanol repeatedly to effectively remove the solvent and unbound 1,6-hexanediamine. Finally, AF-Fe3O4 NPs were dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C.
The FMCH was synthesized via free-radical polymerization. Initially, CS (4.0 g) powder was dissolved in ethanoic acid solution (4 wt. %, 100 mL), and then CNFs-CDs (2.0 mL) were.

added to the CS and ethanoic acid solution (6.0 mL) and mixed evenly. AF-Fe3O4 NPs (0.0200 g) were dispersed in CS and CNFs-CDs solution above mentioned and stirred for 30 min. Monomer Acrylic acid (AA) (2.0 g) was added to the mixture and agitated. Then, NaOH solution (4.45 mL, 5 mol/L) was added to neutralize with AA and then crosslinking agent MBA (0.0100 g) initiated by PPS (0.0050 g) at 60 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere for 1 h. The obtained hydrogel was immersed in water for two days to remove residual reagents. The hydrogels were named FMCH-1, FMCH-2, FMCH-3, FMCH-4, FMCH-5, FMCH-6, FMCH-7, FMCH-8 and FMCH-9, respectively, according to the labels of the CNFs-CDs (1, 2, 3, …, 9). Moreover, the blank chitosan-based hydrogel (BH) was synthesized according to the above procedure without CNFs and AF-Fe3O4 NPs.

A schematic diagram of the fabrication of the chitosan-based hydrogel is shown in Fig. 1. First, CNFs were prepared by TEMPO-mediated oxidation and a hydrothermal reaction was applied to synthesize CDs. Then, CNFs-CDs were fabricated by the condensation reaction using CNFs and CDs as raw materials and blended with CS solution. AF-Fe3O4 NPs were prepared by a solvothermal method using FeCl3 and 1,6-hexanediamine. AF-Fe3O4 NPs were dispersed onto CS and CS could work as an effective stabilizer for AF-Fe3O4 NPs. Afterwards, AA was added to CS/AF-Fe3O4 aqueous and CS/AF-Fe3O4/AA salts were formed via the strong electrostatic interaction of –NH2 (from CS) and –COOH (from AA). With constant stirring and appropriate heat, AA monomers were polymerized in situ with CS and simultaneously covalently cross-linked with MBA. Under heating, the initiator PPS generated sulfate anion radicals which may strip down the hydrogen of –NH2 or –OH groups on CS to form macroradicals. Next, the active sites of macroradicals initiated the copolymerization of CS and AA. CNFs-CDs and AF-Fe3O4 NPs were connected with the CS-AA network via hydrogen bonding. Finally, the FMCH was prepared.

The surface morphology of the prepared nanoparticles and hydrogels was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The crystal structures were obtained by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8). The functional groups of these prepared hydrogels were analysed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet IN10TM, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra of these prepared hydrogels were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS9800, Edinburgh Instruments, England). The magnetic hysteresis loops were investigated by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, LakeShore, 7404, USA). The elemental composition of these hydrogels was examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Sigma Probe, VG Scientific, UK). The Cr (VI) concentration in the solution was determined by ICP-MS (iCAP 6300, Thermo, USA).

The sample preparation process for SEM is as follows. After synthesizing, washing and drying, AF-Fe3O4 nanoparticles were preserved in an anhydrous and vacuum bag, and then they sent for SEM scanning. The obtained FMCH-7 needed to be frozen rapidly by soaking in liquid nitrogen, then FMCH-7 was smashed with a hammer into pieces. Immediately, these pieces were collected and put into the vacuum freeze drier for 36 h to be made into aerogels. These aerogels were kept in anhydrous bag and next sent for observation under optical microscopy and SEM.

The sample preparation process for XRD is as follows. Chitosan and AF-Fe3O4 were ground uniformly with a mortar, then they passed through a 200-mesh sieve and were collected. Since CNFs, CNFs-CDs, and FMCH-7 contained water and were hard to be ground into powder, they were freeze-dried for 36 h, and were pressed into flakes (diameter 2 cm, thickness 1–2 mm). Finally, these samples were all put into the glass sample pool for XRD scanning.

The sample preparation process for FTIR is as follows. Chitosan powder and AF-Fe3O4 particles were taken and pressed into thin flakes (diameter 2 cm, thickness 1–2 mm) with a tablet machine. CNFs, CNFs-CDs, and FMCH-7 were freeze-dried in vacuum freeze drier until there was no moisture, and then pressed into flakes. Ultimately, they were put into the sample plate for FTIR scanning.

The sample preparation process for UV–vis and PL spectra is as follows. Before the FMCH-7 was gelated, the mixed liquid of FMCH-7 was transferred to quartz cuvettes, and then it’s heated to shorten the time of gelation. The samples were stored in dark and taken to the sample tanks of ultraviolet spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrometer for detection. Distilled water was used as the blank control group.
Before the test of VSM, the mass of AF-Fe3O4 and FMCH-7 was weighed with the analytical balance and recorded, then they were placed into the sample tube to measure the hysteresis loop.

Adsorption experiments were performed on the BH and FMCH-7. The prepared hydrogel (10.0 g) and Cr (VI) ion solution (200 mL) were placed into a 250 mL flask. Then, the flask was placed in a water bath shaker with continuous shaking at the speed of 220 rpm. The effects of different factors were investigated in the following experiments: pH (2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8), temperature (15, 25, 35, 45 and 55 °C), Cr (VI) concentrations (40, 80, 120, 160, 200 and 240 mg/L) at the fixed contact time of 48 h, and contact times (5, 20, 40, 80, 120 and 160 min) at the fixed initial concentration of 200 mg/L. The Cr (VI) concentration after sorption was determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). All the sorption experiments were repeated three times. Furthermore, recycling experiments were conducted at pH 5.0 and at room temperature of 25 °C, and NaOH solution (1 mol/L) was used to remove Cr (VI). After the first sorption, the used adsorbent FMCH-7 was placed in a conical flask containing NaOH eluent and oscillated for 2 h. Then the adsorbent was separated by an external magnet, rinsed with deionized water several times and reused in the next regeneration experiment. Five cycles were carried out.

In the FMCH, the main framework is built by CS and AA, while AF-Fe3O4 NPs are dispersed stably by them. CNFs-CDs acted as a natural framework to facilitate the formation of 3D porous structures, which provided mass transfer channels; in addition, CNFs-CDs were used as an optical sensor for the fluorescent detection of heavy metals. Meanwhile, AF-Fe3O4 NPs modified the network structure of the chitosan-based hydrogel and increased its surface area for metal adsorption.

The morphology of AF-Fe3O4 nanoparticles observed by SEM is shown in Fig. 2a. The particles present a spherical shape in the range of 50–110 nm and a rough surface. The porous structure and morphology of the FMCH-7 can be clearly seen in optical microscopy (Fig. 2b) and the SEM (Fig. 2c) images. 3D interconnected porous structures with different sizes were observed in the fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel. In this case, more active sites and ion transport channels were exposed to heavy metal ions, which enhanced adsorption capacity and adsorption rate.

As shown in Fig. 2d, CS, CNFs, CNFs-CDs, FMCH-7 and AF-Fe3O4 were characterized by XRD. AF-Fe3O4 exhibited eight significant evident bands (2θ = 18.2°, 30.0°, 35.3°, 36.9°, 42.9°, 53.3°, 56.8°, 62.4° and 65.7°), corresponding to the crystalline structure of Fe3O4 NPs. These bands belonged to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440) and (531) crystal planes, respectively (JCPDS card No. 19–0629). The results showed that AF-Fe3O4 NPs were successfully synthesized [41]. Furthermore, the sharp and strong peaks of the product revealed its proper crystallinity. Furthermore, the six characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 showed that amino groups did not cause any measurable changes in the phase property of Fe3O4 cores. Therefore, the amino groups were fixed on the surface of Fe3O4 cores, making a core-shell structure. In other words, binding and amino-functionalization (–NH2) occurred only on the surface of Fe3O4 cores to form a core-shell structure [42]. CNFs and CNFs-CDs showed three clear peaks (2θ = 14.9°, 17.0° and 22.7°), corresponding to the crystalline structure of cellulose I [43]. The addition of CDs did not change its properties as a microscopic crystal of cellulose. CS exhibited a broad peak (2θ = 19.8°) which was attributed to the amorphous structure of CS [44]. For FMCH-7, the peaks belonging to CS cannot be clearly observed, which may be due to the blending of CS into the network structure of the chitosan-based hydrogel, which reduced the crystallinity of CS. There was an obvious wide peak near 20°, which may be the superposition of the peaks of CS and CNFs. In addition, FMCH-7 had obvious peaks at 30.0°, 35.3° and 62.4°, which were derived from AF-Fe3O4 NPs, indicating that AF-Fe3O4 NPs were loaded on FMCH-7. Furthermore, three clear peaks (2θ = 22.7°, 17.0° and 14.9°) were not observed in the XRD spectrum of FMCH-7. This may be due to the fact that CNFs-CDs were connected with CS through hydrogen bonding, which reduced the internal hydrogen bonding of the CNFs-CDs chain and destroyed its crystalline structure.

The functional groups of CS, CNFs, CNFs-CDs and FMCH- 7 were determined by FTIR spectroscopy. The results of FTIR spectroscopy are shown in Fig. 2e. In the CS spectrum, the broad band at 3340 cm−1 belonged to the –OH and –NH2 stretching vibrations, and the band at 1560 cm−1 was related to the –NH2 bending vibration [45] and the band associated with –COO− stretching vibration at 1405 cm−1. For CNFs and CNFs-CDs, some typical bands of cellulose species were shown. A new band attributed to the –CO–NH– stretching vibration appeared at 1649 cm−1 for CNFs-CDs, which suggested that CDs were loaded on CNFs [39]. In the case of FMCH-7, the above-mentioned characteristic bands for CS and CNFs-CDs were observed, which indicated that CNFs-CDs were physically attached to the chitosan-based hydrogel and crosslinking reaction of CNFs and CDs occurred. The figures of bare Fe3O4 and AF-Fe3O4 are shown in Fig. S1. A sharp peak of 570 cm−1 indicates Fe3O4 was synthesized. And a band at 1604 cm−1 belonged to the N-H bending vibration, which proved the presence of NH2 on Fe3O4.

The UV–vis spectrum of FMCH-7 is shown in Fig. 2f. There was one small peak at 350 nm from CDs [38]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of FMCH-7 are also illustrated in Fig. 2f. The optimal excitation (354 nm) and emission (508 nm) bands were observed.

The magnetic hysteresis loops of AF-Fe3O4 NPs and FMCH-7 are shown in Fig. 2g. For the AF-Fe3O4 NPs, a slim hysteresis loop with saturation magnetization (Ms = 79.9 emu/g) at 25 °C indicated that the AF-Fe3O4 NPs had good super-paramagnetic properties. For the FMCH-7, the magnetization (Ms = 3.22 emu/g) was much lower than the AF-Fe3O4 NPs. It can be explained as follows: the low content of AF-Fe3O4 NPs in the FMCH compared with the standing Fe3O4, and that, AF-Fe3O4 NPs were clad by the CNFs and CS network, both of which resulted in the weakness of the magnetization of the hydrogel. Although FMCH-7 had a not high saturation magnetization value, it can be separated from the water by Nd-Fe-B permanent magnet (N35 grade), which is shown in the bottom of Fig. 2g, so it has good recyclability in wastewater treatment [53].

This FMCH exhibited cyan fluorescence under UV-light irradiation (365 nm) using a handheld UV lamp. The changes in the fluorescence appearance of FMCH-1, FMCH-2, FMCH-3, FMCH-4, FMCH-5, FMCH-6, FMCH-7, FMCH-8 and FMCH-9 are shown in Fig. 3a. The observed fluorescence was different, and FMCH-7 showed the strongest fluorescence and the absolute quantum yield (AQY) of FMCH-7 was measured to be 2.3 %. Hence all other experiments were performed with FMCH-7.

As a solid-phase fluorescent probe, this FMCH can be used to selectively and sensitively determine Cr (VI) ions. The selectivity was evaluated by testing the fluorescence response of the chitosan-based hydrogel FMCH-7 to various heavy metals (Fig. 3b). During this process, we examined the fluorescence quenching effect of various metal ions (Cu2+, Ba2+, Li+, Cr6+, Na+, K+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cr3+ and Mn6+) at the concentration of 2000 mg/L. Among these tested metal ions, Cr (VI) ions had the greatest effect on fluorescence quenching of FMCH-7, whereas the other metal ions had little effect. There was a significant decay curves in the AQY of FMCH-7 in the presence of Cr (VI) (Fig. 3c). The results confirmed the excellent selectivity of the chitosan-based hydrogel. The high selectivity of Cr (VI) can be explained as follows: Cr (VI) had a broad UV–vis absorption peak around 350–370 nm, which was overlapped by the fluorescence excitation spectrum of FMCH-7 at 354 nm. This confirmed that Cr (VI) had an inner filter effect on FMCH-7. Due to the absorption of Cr (VI), fewer photons arrived at FMCH-7, which resulted in the fluorescence quenching of FMCH-7.

The sensitivity of Cr (VI) detection was further investigated. When the Cr (VI) concentration was increased, a decrease in the continuous fluorescence of FMCH-7 was observed under 365 nm UV. Fig. 3d shows the AQY of FMCH-7 decreased from 2.3 % to 0 % over a range of Cr (VI) concentrations. The linear calibration was in the concentration range of 20–800 mg/L with a regression equation of AQY = −0.0003983 × C0 + 2.286, where C0 is the concentration of Cr (VI) and R [2] is 0.9948. Using the 3σ rule (σ = S0/S, S0 is known as the standard deviation of the blank after repeated measurements, and S is the slope of the calibration curve), the detection limit of Cr (VI) was calculated to be 14.21 mg/L.

To demonstrate the applicability of the hydrogel, FMCH-7 was further used for the analysis of Cr (VI) ions in practical samples (pond and tap water). Since Cr (VI) ions were not originally present in tap water and pond water samples, a standard solution of Cr (VI) was added. As shown in Table 1, the spiked recoveries ranged from 92.2 % to 106.1 %, and the relative standard deviation was less than 14.6 %, indicating that the synthesized FMCH-7 can detect Cr (VI) ions in practical samples.

Fig. 4j shows a visual description of the heavy metal sorption process. Initially, one cylinder of prepared chitosan-based hydrogel was put into the beaker. Then, CuSO4 solution (2000 mg/L) was added under continuous stirring for 10 min; 2 h later, the solution changed from blue to clear. The results showed that the chitosan-based hydrogel had a high adsorption ability for heavy metals.

The effect of pH on Cr (VI) sorption was studied. Fig. 4a shows the change in adsorption capacity with pH. The adsorption capacity increased as the pH increased from 2.0 to 5.0, but decreased as the pH increased further from 5 to 8. When the pH reached 5.0, there was a maximum in the adsorption capacity, which may be due to electrostatic attraction. Hence, the pH was set at 5.0 in the subsequent adsorption experiments. To explore the effect of temperature on the Cr (VI) adsorption, adsorption experiments were performed at various temperatures in the range of 15–55 °C (Fig. 4b). The adsorption capacity of FMCH-7 increased slightly within the above-mentioned range. These results were accordant with the endothermic nature of the adsorption process. For practical application, room temperature (25 °C) was selected in the subsequent experiments.

The effects of initial concentration on Cr (VI) adsorption of FMCH-7 and BH were studied (Fig. 4d). As shown in the figure, the Cr (VI) adsorption of FMCH-7 and BH increased with the increase of Cr (VI) concentration. All equilibrium adsorption capacities of FMCH-7 were greater than those of BH. It was verified that the addition of CNFs-CDs and AF-Fe3O4 NPs enhanced the adsorption ability of FMCH-7. In addition, two common adsorption isotherm models (i.e., Langmuir and Freundlich models) were used to fit the experimental data of FMCH-7 (Fig. 4e and f) [46]. The correlation coefficients (R2) from these two models were 0.8964 and 0.9945, respectively. These figures showed that the Freundlich isotherm model was a better fit to the adsorption data, which indicated the occurrence of multilayer sorption on FMCH-7. Based on the fitting results of the Langmuir model (Fig. 4e), the maximum sorption capacity of FMCH-7 was calculated to be 212.1 mg/g. The maximum sorption capacities of other chitosan-based hydrogels and carbon materials (AC) are summarized in Table 2. The FMCH-7 presented relatively high sorption capacity compared to the previously reported results. It was confirmed that this fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating AF-Fe3O4 NPs and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots was a promising adsorbent.

The effects of contact time on Cr (VI) adsorption of FMCH-7 and BH were researched (Fig. 4g). The figure showed that the Cr (VI) adsorption capacity of FMCH-7 was larger and increased faster than that of BH in the same period. Furthermore, two commonly used kinetic models (i.e., pseudo- first-order and pseudo-second-order models) were used to analyse the adsorption kinetics data of FMCH-7 (Fig. 4h and i) [56]. The sorption kinetics data showed a better fit to the pseudo-second-order model with a higher correlation coefficient than in the case of the pseudo-first-order model. The results indicated that the rate-limiting step of FMCH-7 was chemical adsorption during the sorption process.

Reusability is a vital component of magnetic adsorbents. To research the reusability of FMCH-7, the adsorbent was regenerated and reused for multiple cycles (Fig. 4c). After 5 times cycles, Qe decreased slightly from 212.1 mg/g to 177.3 mg/g. The slight decrease may be caused by the incomplete desorption of Cr (VI) on FMCH-7 in NaOH solution. Thus, the FMCH-7 remained good sorption performance after repeated recycling, making it recyclable and economical.

The characterization results of the prepared fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel showed that this hydrogel exhibited efficient adsorption and detection of Cr (VI). To understand the Cr (VI) adsorption and detection mechanism of the prepared fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel, FTIR and XPS spectra were used.

The FTIR spectra before and after sorption are shown in Fig. 5a. The FTIR spectrum of FMCH-7 before sorption showed the characteristic absorption band of O−H stretching vibrations at 3360 cm−1, the band of −NH2 bending vibration at 1560 cm−1, and the band associated with −COO− stretching vibration at 1405 cm−1. Compared with the FTIR spectrum of FMCH-7 after sorption, the above three bands were reduced; the characteristic absorption band of chromium ions at 904 cm−1 was increased [57]. The above changes confirmed that −OH, −NH2 and −COO− were effective for the adsorption of Cr (VI).

To study the mechanism of FMCH-7, XPS analysis was performed. The total XPS spectrum (Fig. 5b) had four peaks at 284.5, 399.8, 531.0 and 710.5 eV, attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s and Fe2p, respectively. After Cr (VI) adsorption, two new peaks at 579.8 and 589.2 eV appeared, which were related to Cr2p; this indicated that Cr (VI) ions were successfully adsorbed by the fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel. The high-resolution Cr2p spectrum (Fig. 5c) showed that both Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 envelopes were divided into two specific Cr (VI) and Cr (III) peaks. The binding energies at 579.8 and 589.2 eV were related to Cr (VI). Meanwhile, two binding energies at 577.5 and 587.4 eV were associated with Cr (III). This indicated that many Cr (VI) ions were reduced to Cr (III) ions [58], and reduced Cr (III) ions were also attached to the fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel. The high-resolution O1s spectrum of FMCH-7 (Fig. 5d) can be divided into two peaks, corresponding to CO (532.8 eV) and C−O (531.3 eV), respectively. Both peaks showed small movements after the adsorption of Cr (VI) ions, whereas the peaks, corresponding to CO (532.6 eV) and C−O (531.0 eV). This change implied that there was an association between the chromium ions and oxygen atoms in the −OH and −COO− groups. Meanwhile, the N1s spectrum of FMCH-7 obtained after Cr (VI) adsorption, no new peaks were observed, only some movement of the original peak. This result indicated that the amino groups were involved in the Cr (VI) adsorption, and no complexation occurred [59]. Fig. 5f shows the Fe2p spectra of FMCH-7 before and after adsorption. Fe (II) (710.5 eV, 724.2 eV) and Fe (III) (713.8 eV, 728.7 eV) were observed in the FMCH-7, further confirming that AF-Fe3O4 NPs were loaded on the gel. Furthermore, no new peaks were observed, only some movements for these two original peaks. This result confirmed that Cr (VI) ions were attracted by AF-Fe3O4 NPs and removed by Fe (III) and Fe (II) via ion exchange.

Based on the above results, the Cr (VI) adsorption and detection mechanism of the FMCH-7 is as shown in Fig. 6. First, because Cr (VI) ions were easily electrostatically adsorbed on the surface of the hydrogel due to the presence of amino groups, while AF-Fe3O4 NPs had a magnetic attraction and via ion exchange. As the porous structures of the fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel served as the mass transfer channel, Cr (VI) ions quickly moved from the surface to the inside area. Meanwhile, the reduction process of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) occurred through the redox reaction of the existing carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups. The reduced Cr (III) ions were adsorbed on the hydrogel by ion exchange. For these unique structures of the fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel, efficient adsorption of Cr (VI) was obtained. Furthermore, CDs were wrapped in the porous structures of the hydrogel as the optical sensor of Cr (VI). For the same reason of electrostatic attraction, Cr (VI) ions were quickly adsorbed on CDs, which resulted in fluorescence quenching. Based on the fast visual response to Cr (VI), the fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel showed high-efficiency detection for Cr (VI).

For practical applications in industry, the cost of adsorbents is one of the most important features. The cost of FMCH-7 production was estimated. According to the cost-estimation procedures reported in literature [60], the breakup cost of each step and the total cost were summarised in Table S1. The total cost for the preparation of 1 kg of FMCH-7 was estimated as CNY 118.57. Meanwhile, activated carbon (AC) can be purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (China) at a cost of CNY 269.6 per kg. In comparison with AC, the cost of FMCH-7 adsorbent is much lower. Furthermore, the excellent reusability of FMCH-7 and convenient operation would further reduce the use-cost. Hence, considering its environmental and financial benefits, FMCH-7 was a promising adsorbent for removing Cr (VI).

In this study, a fluorescent magnetic chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating AF-Fe3O4 NPs and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots was prepared using a simple, cost-effective and environmentally friendly method for the removal of Cr (VI) ions from aqueous solution and was characterized by SEM, XRD, FTIR, UV–vis, PL and VSM. The adsorption equilibrium isotherms and kinetics showed that the adsorption in this hydrogel followed the Freundlich and pseudo-second-order models better, and the maximum sorption capacity for Cr (VI) ions was 212.1 mg/g at 25 °C and pH 5.0. After 5 adsorption cycles, FMCH still maintained 83.6 % of the initial adsorption capacity. Furthermore, the FMCH-7 had a detection limit of 14.2 mg/L with an analytical linear range of 20–800 mg/L for the selective and sensitive determination of Cr (VI). The XPS and FTIR analyses were long-established that the addition of CNFs-CDs and AF-Fe3O4 NPs could build porous structures and mass transfer channels and provide more binding sites and rapid visual response to Cr (VI), leading to significant improvements in Cr (VI) adsorption and detection. Overall, the FMCH-7 is considered a promising adsorbent for the removal of Cr (VI) ions because of its high adsorption capacity and ability to detect Cr (VI) ions.
